author: doll-agent
content:
Research outcome: keep element-counted strides, decline the byte-stride refactor.

The constraint: numpy's byte strides enable dtype-erased pointer arithmetic on char* buffers. ferray inherits ndarray's element-counted strides; dtype-erased iteration goes through DynArray's match-over-variants.

Why byte strides would cost more than they save:
1. ferray's Element trait is sealed; the universe is closed and fully enumerable. DynArray::iter dispatches once per call - match cost amortizes across the iteration.
2. Element-typed strides are the basis for all SIMD dispatch (pulp lane counts, contiguous-flat shortcuts). Byte strides would either duplicate the stride machinery or require recomputing element strides at every SIMD entry.
3. Real numpy use cases (buffer-protocol export, type-erased C interop) already have first-class workarounds: ferray-numpy-interop produces correctly-typed PyArray<T,D>, ascontiguousarray is per-variant, copyto-from-untyped is dtype-level memcpy.

What would change the calculus: a future feature that needs erased aggregation (concatenate across DynArrays) or direct Python buffer-protocol ABI without going through PyArray would tip the balance.

For now: closed-Element + DynArray dispatch is the cheaper bet. Closing as decided.
